items.q: What does this church teach about hope after a believer dies?
items.a: From Pastor Calvert's sermon series on hope: the believer's hope rests on three immovable things — that Christ himself rose bodily from the dead as a historical event attested by eyewitnesses; that the believer is united to Christ by faith, so what is true of the head will be true of every member of his body; and that the Spirit indwells the believer as a deposit guaranteeing what is to come [1]. None of these rests on the believer's strength. When a believer dies, the soul is at once in the presence of Christ (Phil 1:23, 'to depart and be with Christ, which is far better'). The body sleeps in the dust until the resurrection, when at the last trumpet the dead in Christ are raised imperishable [1]. The reunion is bodily and forever.

items.q: What does Cornerstone confess?
items.a: Cornerstone is a confessional Reformed Baptist congregation that holds to the 1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith [1]. We are congregationally governed and elder-led. We were planted in 2014 by seven families and three vocational elders [1].

items.q: Why do you baptize believers and not infants?
items.a: From the March 2024 sermon on baptism: we baptize on profession of faith because the New Testament order is consistently belief first, then baptism — the Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8, the Philippian jailer in Acts 16, the household of Cornelius in Acts 10. Baptism is the credible profession of a regenerate person being added to the visible church, not a sign administered to those who cannot yet believe [from the sermon archive]. We honour our paedobaptist brothers and sisters but cannot administer the sign in a way our consciences before scripture cannot support.

items.q: How do I become a member of the church?
items.a: Membership at Cornerstone is meaningful, not nominal. The path is: attend regularly for at least three months, complete the membership class, meet with the elders for an interview about your testimony and convictions, then be received before the congregation. Members are expected to know one another and to walk together under elder oversight [1]. For specific timing, contact the office or one of the elders directly.

items.q: When do you meet?
items.a: Lord's Day worship is Sunday morning. For the current service times, parking, and what to expect on a first visit, see the Visit page or contact the office — those details change occasionally and the assistant doesn't try to be the source of truth for them. The mid-week prayer meeting and small groups vary by season [1].

items.q: What does the church believe about scripture?
items.a: We confess the 66 books of the Old and New Testaments to be the inspired, inerrant, sufficient, and final authority for faith and practice. We preach expositionally — book by book — because the text shapes the church, not the other way around [1].

items.q: What is your view of the Lord's Supper?
items.a: We observe the Lord's Supper on the first Lord's Day of each month. The table is open to baptized believers in good standing in a gospel-preaching church. We confess the Reformed Baptist understanding of the Supper as a means of grace by which Christ is spiritually present to and feeds his people by faith — not a re-sacrifice and not a bare memorial [from the sermon archive].

items.q: Do you have small groups?
items.a: Yes — small groups (we call them Care Groups) meet in homes throughout the week and are how members know one another and bear one another's burdens between Lord's Days. New attenders are welcome to visit any group; for help finding one near you, contact the office or speak with an elder after the service [1].

items.q: What's your position on women in ministry?
items.a: We hold to the historic complementarian position confessed by the 1689: men and women are equal in dignity and value as image-bearers, and the offices of elder and pastor are reserved for qualified men. Women serve visibly in many other vital roles — teaching other women and children, hospitality, mercy ministries, and music. For the full pastoral treatment of this question, ask one of the elders or see the position paper available from the office [1].

items.q: I'm grieving — can someone from the church talk to me?
items.a: Yes, and please reach out. The assistant on this page is not a pastor and not a chaplain — for grief, crisis, or any pastoral need, we want a real elder to walk with you. Use the Contact page to reach the office; one of the elders will follow up within the day. If this is an emergency, please call 911 or go to your nearest ER first.

items.q: How do I get involved in serving at Cornerstone?
items.a: We're glad you're interested in serving. Our philosophy of ministry is that every member is a minister. To get involved, start by attending a membership class, where you'll learn about our values and ministries. Then, speak with an elder or a ministry leader about where you can serve. We have opportunities in areas like hospitality, outreach, and discipleship.

items.q: What's the best way to stay up-to-date on church news and events?
items.a: We recommend attending the weekly worship service, where important announcements are made. You can also speak with an elder or contact the office to be added to our email list or to find out about other ways to stay informed.

items.q: Can you help me with my spiritual struggles?
items.a: We care about your spiritual well-being, but for personal struggles, we encourage you to speak with one of our elders or pastors. They are equipped to provide guidance and support. Our role is to provide information about the church and its teachings, not to offer personal spiritual direction.

items.q: How does Cornerstone approach outreach and evangelism?
items.a: We believe that outreach and evangelism are essential parts of our ministry. Our approach is centered on building relationships with our neighbors and sharing the gospel in a clear and respectful way. We also support various missions and outreach efforts locally and globally.

items.q: What is your view on social justice?
items.a: While we recognize the importance of social justice, our primary focus as a church is on proclaiming the gospel and making disciples. For discussions on how our faith informs our social engagement, we recommend speaking with our elders or exploring resources from our sermon archive and recommended readings.

items.q: Can I get married at Cornerstone?
items.a: Yes, we would be honored to host your wedding. However, to be married at Cornerstone, at least one of the spouses must be a member of the church. Additionally, our pastors and elders are involved in the wedding process to ensure that the ceremony is conducted in a way that honors God and our church's values.

items.q: How does the church handle conflicts or disagreements?
items.a: We believe that conflicts are inevitable but can also be opportunities for growth and deeper understanding. Our approach is to address conflicts in a biblical and gracious manner, following the principles outlined in Matthew 18. If you're experiencing a conflict, we encourage you to speak with an elder or pastor.

items.q: What role do women play in the church?
items.a: Women are valued and integral members of our church community. While our church holds to a complementarian view of gender roles in church leadership, women serve in various ministries, including teaching, discipleship, and outreach. We believe that every member, regardless of gender, has a vital role to play in the body of Christ.

items.q: How do you ensure the privacy of personal data?
items.a: We take the privacy and security of personal data very seriously. Our systems are designed to protect your information, and we adhere to best practices in data handling and storage. For more detailed information on our privacy policies, you can contact the office or speak with an elder.

items.q: Can I schedule a private meeting with a pastor?
items.a: Yes, our pastors are available to meet with you. However, due to their schedules, it's best to contact the office to arrange a meeting. Please note that for personal or sensitive matters, we may encourage you to meet with a specific pastor or elder who is best equipped to assist you.

items.q: How does Cornerstone support missions and missionaries?
items.a: We are committed to supporting missions and missionaries locally and globally. Our approach is to partner with missionaries and organizations that share our values and theological convictions. We also have a missions committee that oversees our missions efforts and provides updates to the church.

items.q: What is the process for becoming an elder at Cornerstone?
items.a: Becoming an elder at Cornerstone involves a careful process of nomination, evaluation, and training. Elders must demonstrate a deep understanding of our church's confession of faith, a strong character, and a willingness to serve. The process is outlined in our church's bylaws and is overseen by our existing elders.

items.q: Can you explain the church's stance on creation and evolution?
items.a: Our church confesses that God created the heavens and the earth and all that is in them. For a detailed explanation of our views on creation and evolution, we recommend consulting our confession of faith or speaking with one of our elders or pastors. They can provide more insight into how we understand these topics in light of Scripture.

items.q: How does the church approach church discipline?
items.a: Church discipline is a serious and sensitive topic. Our approach is guided by Scripture, particularly Matthew 18 and 1 Corinthians 5. We aim to restore and redeem, rather than merely punish. If you have questions or concerns about church discipline, we encourage you to speak with an elder or pastor.

items.q: What resources does the church offer for biblical counseling?
items.a: We believe in the sufficiency of Scripture for addressing the complexities of life. While we don't provide professional counseling, our elders and trained members offer biblical counseling and discipleship. For more information, contact the office or speak with an elder about how to get connected with these resources.

items.q: Can you help me understand the difference between your church and others?
items.a: We're happy to discuss our distinctives and what sets us apart. However, for comparative analyses of different denominations or theological traditions, we recommend exploring our sermon archive or speaking with an elder. Our focus is on proclaiming and living out our confession of faith.

items.q: How does Cornerstone view the role of the Holy Spirit in the life of the believer?
items.a: We believe the Holy Spirit is the Comforter and Helper promised by Jesus, who applies the work of Christ to believers and equips them for service. For a deeper understanding of our pneumatology, we recommend consulting our confession of faith or listening to relevant sermons from our archive.

items.q: What is the architectural philosophy behind your church building?
items.a: Our church building is designed to facilitate worship, fellowship, and ministry. The architecture reflects our commitment to simplicity, functionality, and beauty, aiming to create an environment that honors God and serves the body of Christ.

items.q: Can I request a specific song or hymn for a service?
items.a: While we appreciate your interest in our worship music, our song selections are carefully chosen by our worship team to align with the sermon and the liturgy of the service. We aim to create a cohesive and meaningful worship experience. However, you can certainly make suggestions, and we'll consider them for future services.

items.q: How does the church approach technology and social media?
items.a: We recognize the importance and influence of technology and social media in today's world. Our approach is to use these tools in a way that supports our mission and values, while also being mindful of the potential pitfalls and distractions they can pose.
